blocks.content: Coordinated Workflows & Sync Pipelines

While each of the three interfaces has its distinct strength, they are not mutually exclusive. The true power of the Google Antigravity ecosystem lies in coordinating all three surfaces. Because they all interface with the same underlying engine, they share a single workspace source of truth.
The Shared Directory State

The central point of sync is the project folder itself. This folder contains the .gemini/ database cache, local files, and .gemini.md governance rules. Let's look at how tasks started in one interface are visible and modifiable in others:
- Visual outputs: Files generated by a web-scraping card in antigravity appear immediately in the file tree of antigravity-ide.
- Workspace boundaries: Security rules and folder locks set up via CLI (agy) are strictly enforced in both the visual board and the editor.
- Session history: Agent activity logs and tool calls can be monitored using terminal commands, visual cards, or the editor sidecar.
Real-world Scenario: Research to Code Delivery
This scenario walks through a complete end-to-end development cycle utilizing all three surfaces:

- Step 1: Web Research: Open antigravity (Visual Board) and run a research card that scrapes documentation tables from standard public APIs. The agent writes this raw scraped data to a workspace file called api_ref.json.
- Step 2: Verification: Run 'agy --verify' in your terminal. This CLI check confirms that the newly created api_ref.json file is syntactically valid and complies with workspace rule sizes.

- Step 3: Code Co-Authoring: Open antigravity-ide. Open a python file where you want to implement the API calls. Select the code region, open the Sidecar prompt, reference api_ref.json, and ask the agent to generate the helper functions.
- Step 4: Refactoring: Review the proposed code diff inside the editor, accept the changes, and execute the tests using the integrated terminal.
> [!WARNING]

> Concurrency Warning: Do not run concurrent write operations on the same file across different interfaces (e.g., trying to modify a file with an inline rewrite in antigravity-ide while a CLI agent is writing to it). While the engine maintains locks, concurrent updates can cause local version conflicts.
Interface Selection Summary Checksheet
Use this quick checksheet to select the optimal tool for any task:
- Select CLI (agy) when: Running rapid file mutations, scripting bulk file tasks, running status diagnostics, or executing shell pipes.
- Select Standalone (antigravity) when: Orchestrating multi-agent tasks, running visual web research, monitoring browser automation playbacks, or managing task lists.
- Select IDE (antigravity-ide) when: Writing code or documents, running line-by-line diff inspections, co-authoring text, or requiring chat guidance.
Check Your Understanding
Complete this final quiz on cross-interface workflows:
- Question 1: What is the primary common file that defines the workspace boundaries and security rule boundaries for all three interfaces?
- A) package.json
- B) .gemini.md
- C) Dockerfile
Feedback: Option B is correct. The .gemini.md file defines directory boundaries, file permissions, and tool access limits for all interfaces connected to the workspace.
- Question 2: If an agent writes a file using a Visual Board card in antigravity, how do you access that file in antigravity-ide?
- A) You must export it via a web download link on the card.
- B) The file is written directly to the shared local workspace folder and is immediately visible in the IDE's file tree.
- C) You must sync the IDE database using a CLI command.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Because all interfaces operate on the same local workspace directory, any file written by one interface is instantly accessible to the others.
